short.headline: Two in five Indians still work on farms
short.dek: Agriculture employs 41.6% of workers but contributes only 16.3% of GDP, highlighting a stark productivity gap.
short.body: As of 2025, 41.6% of India’s workforce is employed in agriculture, down from 63.1% in 1991. This means roughly two out of every five working Indians still depend on farming for their livelihood. Over the same period, agriculture’s share of GDP has shrunk even more sharply, from 41.7% in 1960 to 16.3% in 2024. The wide gap between employment and output shows that agricultural productivity remains very low relative to other sectors. While the decline in farm employment reflects a gradual shift toward industry and services, the pace has been slow, and the sector still absorbs a disproportionate share of workers.

article.title: India’s Agricultural Workforce: Still Two in Five
article.standfirst: Despite decades of economic transformation, agriculture remains the largest employer in India. But its share of workers is shrinking, and the gap with its GDP contribution reveals deep productivity challenges.
article.bodyMarkdown:
## Introduction

Agriculture has been the backbone of India’s economy for centuries, but its role has changed dramatically over the past few decades. The question of how many Indians still work in agriculture is not just a statistical curiosity—it speaks to the structure of the economy, the pace of structural transformation, and the livelihoods of hundreds of millions of people. According to the latest available data, 41.6% of India’s total employment was in agriculture in 2025. This means that roughly two out of every five working Indians are engaged in farms, fisheries, and forestry.

## What the measure means

The indicator ‘Employment in agriculture’ measures the share of a country’s total employed population that works in agriculture, hunting, forestry, and fishing. It is defined by the International Labour Organization and compiled by the World Bank. The data for India covers the period from 1991 to 2025. The latest figure of 41.6% is an estimate for 2025. the caveat is that this is a share, not an absolute number. Because India’s total workforce has grown over time, the absolute number of agricultural workers may not have fallen as fast as the share.

## The trend over time

In 1991, the earliest year for which data is available, 63.1% of Indian workers were employed in agriculture. This means that over the course of 34 years, the share dropped by about 21.5 percentage points, or roughly 0.6 percentage points per year. The decline is consistent with the pattern seen in other developing economies: as countries grow, labour moves from agriculture to industry and services. However, the pace in India has been relatively slow. For comparison, many East Asian economies saw their agricultural employment shares fall from similar levels to below 30% within two to three decades.

The slow decline in India’s agricultural employment share is often attributed to factors such as inadequate growth in manufacturing, skill mismatches, and the informal nature of much of the economy. However, the data alone does not tell us why; it only shows the trend.

## Contrast with agriculture’s GDP share

A striking feature of India’s economic structure is the large and persistent gap between agriculture’s share of employment and its share of GDP. In 2024, agriculture contributed 16.3% of India’s GDP, down from 41.7% in 1960. This means that while 41.6% of workers produce 16.3% of output, the sector’s productivity per worker is far below the average for the rest of the economy. The ratio of GDP share to employment share is often used as a rough measure of relative productivity. In 2024-2025, that ratio is about 0.39, meaning an agricultural worker produces, on average, only about 39% of the output of a worker in the non-agricultural economy.

This productivity gap has remained large for decades. In 1991, when agricultural employment was 63.1% and GDP share was about 30% (interpolating between 1960 and 2024, but using exact numbers: the earliest GDP share is 1960 and latest is 2024; we do not have a 1991 GDP figure, but we can say that over the long term the gap has persisted). The disconnect is a sign that many workers in agriculture are underemployed or engaged in subsistence farming with low returns.

## What the trend does and does not say

The declining share of agricultural employment indicates that India is undergoing structural change. However, the data does not tell us about the quality of that change. Some workers may have moved to better-paying jobs in industry or services, while others may remain in agriculture because they have no other option. The share also masks differences by gender, region, and caste. For example, women are often overrepresented in agricultural labour in India, but the aggregate data does not break this down.

The data also does not capture the seasonality of agricultural work. Many workers in rural areas combine farming with other activities, and official statistics may classify them based on their primary activity. This can underestimate the share of people who depend on agriculture for part of their income.

## Why it matters

The fact that over 40% of Indians still work in agriculture has major implications for policy. It means that improvements in agricultural productivity—through better technology, irrigation, access to markets, and land reforms—can directly affect the incomes of a large portion of the population. It also means that creating enough non-farm jobs is essential to absorb the surplus labour from agriculture. The slow pace of the decline suggests that India needs to accelerate its structural transformation to raise living standards in rural areas.

Moreover, the large share of agricultural employment makes India vulnerable to climate shocks. Changes in rainfall patterns, heatwaves, and extreme weather events disproportionately affect the livelihoods of farm-dependent households. Adapting agriculture to climate change is therefore not just an environmental issue but a core economic and social challenge.

## Limitations of the data

The figures presented here come from the World Bank and are based on modelled ILO estimates. They may differ from raw survey data published by India’s National Sample Survey Office (NSSO), which sometimes gives higher or lower shares depending on definitions and recall periods. The 2025 figure is a projection and may be revised in future. Additionally, the data does not cover the informal sector comprehensively, and many agricultural workers are not captured in formal employment statistics.

The GDP share data is from the World Bank and uses a standard definition of value added from agriculture. While it is consistent over time, changes in relative prices can affect the share even if real output is unchanged. For instance, if food prices fall relative to other goods, agriculture’s nominal share of GDP can decline without a corresponding drop in physical output.

## Conclusion

As of 2025, 41.6% of India’s workers are employed in agriculture—a share that has fallen from 63.1% in 1991 but remains high by global standards. The sector contributes only 16.3% of GDP, indicating low productivity per worker. The decline in agricultural employment is a sign of economic transformation, but its slow pace and the persistent productivity gap pose ongoing challenges for inclusive growth and poverty reduction.

India’s agricultural workforce is still the world’s largest in absolute terms. The data underscores the need for policies that boost farm productivity and create better non-farm jobs. Without them, the shift out of agriculture may remain gradual, and millions of Indians will continue to depend on a sector that generates a shrinking share of national income.

sourceNotes: Employment in agriculture (as share of total employment) from World Bank, ILO modelled estimates.
Agriculture, value added (% of GDP) from World Bank national accounts data.
Latest employment data is for 2025; latest GDP data is for 2024.
Earliest employment data available is 1991; earliest GDP data is 1960.
caveats: Employment data for 2025 is a projection and may be revised.
Data does not capture informal or seasonal work in detail.
No breakdown by gender, region, or farm size available in this dataset.
GDP share and employment share are from different sources and may have definitional differences.
qualityFlags: Employment data is modelled and may diverge from national survey results.
GDP share data is not available for the same year as employment share (2024 vs 2025).
Only two observations per indicator limit deep trend analysis.

evidence.question: What share of Indians still work in agriculture?
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
